name: Replit API
what_it_does: Provides programmatic management of Replit Repls (cloud coding environments) including creation, deployment, execution, and hosting of applications.
use_cases: Programmatically create and execute code snippets in isolated sandboxes for safe agent code evaluation
Deploy a generated web application to a public URL without managing server infrastructure
List and inspect an organization's Repls to audit active projects and resource consumption
Trigger a Repl run to verify that generated code executes without errors
Manage Replit Teams deployments for an organization's shared projects via API
not_for: Production-grade workloads requiring guaranteed SLA uptime — Replit is optimized for prototyping
Running computationally intensive ML training jobs — resource limits are strict on free and base paid tiers
Accessing file system state of a Repl programmatically in real time — the API does not expose a file tree endpoint
best_when: An agent needs a zero-setup sandboxed execution environment for untrusted or generated code with an instantly shareable URL.
avoid_when: You require persistent state, low latency, or production SLAs, as Replit Repls sleep after inactivity and wake times can be significant.

auth.notes: Replit uses API tokens tied to a user account. Scoping is all-or-nothing at the account level; there are no fine-grained permission scopes. Store tokens with care.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Free Repls available but with limited compute and storage; Replit Core starts at ~$20/month
pricing.paid_tiers: Replit Core (~$20/month) adds more compute, private Repls, and deployments; Teams plans for organizations
pricing.notes: Free tier is functional for light agent use; deployments and always-on Repls require a paid plan.

performance.rate_limits: Not publicly documented; GraphQL API subject to undocumented rate limits that surface as generic errors

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: GraphQL error responses are inconsistent — some return user-friendly messages, others return internal identifiers. REST endpoint errors are sparse.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Creating a Repl with the same name creates a duplicate with an auto-suffixed name rather than returning the existing resource. Agents must check for existence before creating.
known_agent_gotchas: The public API surface is minimal and largely undocumented — most automation relies on reverse-engineered GraphQL calls that can break without notice
Repl names are not globally unique per user — duplicate names are silently suffixed, which can cause agent state confusion
Repls sleep after inactivity and wake on first request; wake latency (5-30s) must be accounted for in agent workflows
There is no webhook for Repl execution completion — agents must poll or use long-polling patterns
API token scopes are account-wide with no least-privilege option, making token compromise high-impact

security_score_components.security_notes: No scope granularity on API tokens is a significant risk for agent use. All tokens are full-account access. TLS enforced throughout.
